Name: spectral_quantization_routing
Title: Spectral Quantization Routing for Efficient Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free precision allocation via activation covariance geometry.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in mixed-precision inference, such as Spectral Latent Steering and Geometric Signal Separation. However, our approach differs in that it dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry, rather than relying on static profiling or costly calibration.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, SQR maps these geometric diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SQR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate SQR on WILDS benchmarks, comparing its performance to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Assess the impact of spectral conditioning on generalization stability under quantization noise, using PAC-Bayes bounds.
Investigate the effect of different precision-switching policies on latency and accuracy, using a range of latency budgets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may not generalize well to all types of neural networks or inference tasks.
The computational overhead of computing local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates may be significant, potentially impacting latency.
